interest: The interest behind this argument is that of disadvantaged pupils, general-school teachers, and equity-oriented and labour-market advocates who need any compensation mechanism to be durable and evidence-backed before it can be relied upon as a substitute for structural change.
value: The value in tension is equity and durability of provision versus the political-economy pragmatism of a low-friction, non-structural reform that keeps existing institutional arrangements intact.
fear: The driving fear is that compensation will be defunded in the first fiscal-consolidation round and will meanwhile permanently legitimise early sorting, so disadvantaged children get neither structural reform nor durable support.
affected: Roma and rural pupils facing early selection
general-school teachers and teachers in disadvantaged schools
assumption: The claim rests on the unstated assumption that Portugal's TEIP-style gains are the closest available precedent and that their non-transfer to Hungary's age-10 selection context makes offsetting (as opposed to masking) empirically unsupported.
empirical_uncertainty: The factual part is partly settled and partly unknown: the fiscal-fragility of targeted funding is well-documented in Hungarian budgetary history, while the absence of any country demonstrating SES-offsetting at Hungary's selection age is a genuine evidentiary gap (Portugal did not alter its selection age and PISA-gain attribution to TEIP is not separately isolated).
